title: Running a Meeting in English: Key Phrases and Structure
description: Need to lead a meeting in English without freezing? Follow Ana as she opens the call, sets the agenda, manages discussion, and wraps up with action items. Practice English vocabulary for professional meetings with instant word translations.
text:
At 9:58 on a rainy Tuesday in São Paulo, Ana refreshed the video call for the third time. She had been promoted two weeks earlier, and now she was chairing her first meeting in English with colleagues in Tokyo, Seoul, and Berlin. Her slides were ready. Her agenda was not. The real worry was not vocabulary for “budget” or “timeline.” It was the moment when everyone joined, waited, and expected her to lead.

At 10:00, small squares appeared. “Good morning,” someone said. Ana smiled, then heard the silence that follows when nobody knows who should speak next. She remembered the simplest structure her manager had once used and decided to copy it.

“Hi everyone, thanks for joining,” Ana said. “Can you hear me okay?” A few nods. “Great. I’ll give everyone a minute to join, then we’ll start.” That tiny sentence bought her time and signaled control.

When the last person arrived, she named the meeting. “Let’s get started. The goal of today’s meeting is to agree on the Q2 launch date and next steps.” She added a time boundary because English meetings love clocks. “We have 30 minutes, so I’ll keep us moving.” Then she showed one slide with three lines. “Here’s the agenda: first, quick updates; second, the launch options; third, decisions and actions. Does that work for everyone?” Nobody objected, which in meetings often means yes.

Five minutes in, the first problem arrived. Ken from Tokyo spoke softly and used long pauses. Jisoo from Seoul jumped in quickly, finishing Ken’s sentence. Ana saw Ken’s face freeze. She intervened with a phrase that sounded polite but acted like a traffic light. “Sorry to interrupt, Jisoo. Ken, could you finish your point?” Ken continued. Then Ana summarized, so nobody could pretend they hadn’t understood. “So, Ken’s concern is that the supplier lead time is six weeks. Did I get that right?” Ken said yes.

As the discussion moved to dates, people began to circle the same arguments. Ana felt the meeting slipping. She used another chairing tool: a checkpoint. “Let me pause us for a second,” she said. “I’m hearing two options: June 10 or June 24. Let’s look at pros and cons quickly.” The word “hearing” mattered. It told the group she was listening, and it allowed her to reframe the conversation without blaming anyone.

Then the hard moment: disagreement. Berlin wanted speed; Tokyo wanted certainty. Voices stayed polite, but the pace sped up and overlaps started. Ana used a structure her company’s L&D team had taught in a workshop based on common facilitation practice: slow it down, name the issue, ask for data.

“Let’s take this one at a time,” she said. “Berlin, can you share the main reason for June 10, and what would have to be true for it to work?” After the answer, she switched. “Tokyo, what would you need to feel confident about June 10?” Now the conflict was not “us versus them,” but conditions and evidence.

With five minutes left, Ana moved from discussion to decision. “Okay, can we align on a decision?” she asked. Silence again. So she offered a clear proposal. “My suggestion is June 24 as the launch date, with a checkpoint on May 20. If the supplier confirms earlier delivery, we revisit June 10. Any strong objections?” Nobody spoke. In meeting language, that was consensus.

Now she had to protect the decision from disappearing. She assigned actions out loud, because people forget what they only read later. “Great. Let’s confirm next steps. Ken, you’ll ask the supplier for a written lead-time update by Friday. Jisoo, you’ll draft the revised timeline by Monday. Berlin team, you’ll update marketing milestones. Is everyone okay with that?” Each person said yes.

At 10:29, Ana ended the meeting the way strong chairs do: with a clean close. “To recap, we agreed on June 24, with a May 20 checkpoint. I’ll send notes and action items today. Thanks, everyone.” When the squares vanished, Ana noticed something she hadn’t expected. Her English had not been perfect. But her structure had been clear, and the phrases had done their job: start, guide, summarize, decide, and close. The next meeting was already on the calendar.

cardDescription: Learn practical English phrases for opening meetings, setting agendas, moving between topics, managing discussion, and confirming action items.
